what_it_does: CSS stylesheet that implements shared product-chrome components for a gallery/application surface: phone-shaped device frames, standalone screen canvases, and several data-driven screen archetypes (unit view, piece-in-focus, decision card, generic archetype, explained presentation, composed decision view), all styled with design-system CSS custom properties.
observations: Uses extensive CSS custom properties prefixed --dna- plus theme variables such as --warm, --cool, --gold, --ochre, --charcoal.
Defines .phone sizes (default, lg, sm) and a .screen canvas (390×844) as first-class gallery artifacts.
Provides component classes for status bars, headers, prose, stat bands, ledger rows, plates/insets, actions, glass rails, hotspots, hatch patterns, and filter pills.
Includes .screen.uv immersive unit view with warm radial background, frosted card, glass stat chips, and an animated corner V-hub with fanned verb sockets.
Includes .screen.piece preview card with takes and dials.
Includes .screen.decision-card and .ar-* generic archetype with tappable option buttons, recommended/chosen states, error banner, decided moment, ghost action, and empty/failed states.
Implements responsive behavior for .screen.ar.ar-responsive via @media: portrait default, landscape grid at >=760px, and desktop at >=1180px.
Includes .ex-* explained-presentation layout (mockup left, content right) and .dv-* composed decision view.
Uses color-mix, backdrop-filter, repeating-linear-gradient, keyframe animations, and a prefers-reduced-motion guard.

Comments reference external records/contracts: dna/application.json, modes.json, DECISION-CARD-HOST-CONTRACT.md, surface/runtime/archetype.js, DNA.renderUnit, DNA.renderPiece, DNA.renderDecision, DNA.renderArchetype, DNA.renderExplained.
standouts: The .ar-* generic archetype is framed as the one unified renderer every surface uses, resolving form factor by viewport media queries rather than a JS isMobile flag.
Decision options are styled as explicit tappable buttons with a persistent gold 'Choose ›' chevron or filled action bar, emphasizing affordance over info cards.
The .screen.uv V-hub uses CSS transitions and custom properties (--edx, --edy, --i) to fan a radial socket menu from a corner hub with tap-away-to-close behavior.
Strong reliance on design tokens and color-mix for live theming across all archetypes.
conventions: BEM-ish class naming: p- prefix for phone components, screen-scoped overrides, ar- for archetype, ex- for explained, dv- for composed decision view.
CSS variables for spacing, type, radius, and color; only a few structural sizes are hard-coded (e.g., 300px, 390px).
Comments serve as design documentation, citing records, modes, contracts, and renderer functions.
Mobile-first responsive: base portrait styles, then min-width media queries.
Pseudo-elements (::before) carry pattern overlays such as the hatch and band stripes.
concerns: The file is very long and mixes multiple distinct screen archetypes and presentation layouts in one stylesheet, increasing coupling surface area.
It depends on CSS custom properties not defined here (e.g., --dna-space-*, --dna-type-*, --warm, --gold) with no fallbacks, so it requires an external token layer to render correctly.
Many absolute-positioned UI pieces (e.g., .p-act, .uv-words, .uv-node, .uv-chips) use fixed offsets that may drift if tokens or screen sizes change.
The .dv-title rule is truncated in the provided excerpt, leaving an incomplete CSS block.
notes: A token-driven product-chrome stylesheet that doubles as a gallery component library and a set of data-driven screen archetypes, with strong responsive behavior for the generic decision renderer.
questions: Which file defines the referenced CSS custom properties (--dna-*, theme colors) and ensures they are in scope when this stylesheet loads?
Are the .phone and .screen dimensions (300×600, 390×844) intended as canonical device-art sizes or only gallery presentation sizes?
Is the .screen.ar.ar-responsive media-query logic meant to apply inside a real app viewport, or only within the gallery/presentation stage?
What is the intended completion of the truncated .dv-title rule?
How are the --edx, --edy, and --i custom properties for socket fan positions set: inline styles, JS, or another stylesheet?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to provide shared product-chrome components for the application face, driven by design tokens and reused like organisms.js. The actual content matches that for phone/screen shells and reusable component classes, but also expands into full data-driven screen archetypes and presentation layouts (decision card, explained view, composed decision view), so the scope is broader than generic chrome alone.
apparent_themes: Design-token-driven theming and live color adaptation.
Reusable component archetypes over one-off screens.
Clear affordance and signal economy (gold marks recommendation, ochre marks chosen, faint for non-recommended).
Immersive, warm, editorial visual language with frosted glass and radial gradients.
intention_signals: The file appears to be trying to establish a single, shared CSS substrate for all product-facing gallery/app screens so that new pieces can be rendered by composing existing classes rather than writing bespoke CSS, while keeping the visual language warm, token-driven, and responsive across phone, screen, and presentation contexts.
novelty: The combination of a generic archetype renderer (.ar-*) that adapts across portrait/landscape/desktop purely via CSS media queries, plus the corner-hub radial verb wheel (.uv-vee) and the explained/composed decision-view layouts, all within one shared stylesheet tied to a design-token system.
contribution: It contributes a consolidated, token-driven visual vocabulary for phone/screen shells and reusable screen archetypes, including responsive decision-card composition, immersive unit-view chrome, and presentation layouts, which a fused system could keep as a shared styling layer for gallery and app surfaces.
summary_for_embedding: A CSS component library for a product gallery surface defining phone frames, standalone screen canvases, and data-driven screen archetypes (unit view, decision card, generic archetype, explained/composed views) styled entirely with design-system CSS custom properties.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a single reusable, token-driven stylesheet that renders product chrome and data-driven screen archetypes across phone, screen, and presentation contexts without per-piece bespoke CSS.
proposed_edge_kinds: styles_for: stylesheet styles components rendered by JS
implements_contract: CSS implements a documented host contract
token_consumes: stylesheet depends on token file for custom properties
archetype_of: generic archetype specializes concrete screen types
